---
title: "Vertical climbing assay rig"
---

[← Back to hardware](../../hardware.qmd){.back-link}

This rig was built to measure *Drosophila* climbing under optogenetic control, for the mushroom-body
output neuron (MBON) work in my thesis. A climbing assay reads out motor function through negative
geotaxis: knock a fly to the floor of a vertical chamber and it climbs, and how it climbs, its speed,
height, falls and pauses, reports on the motor circuitry driving the behavior. The classic version of
this assay is done by hand, tapping a vial of flies and counting how many pass a line in a set time.
That is fine for catching gross motor deficits, but it could not do what I needed: agitate every fly
the same way, every trial, and hand off cleanly to timed light stimulation and frame-by-frame tracking.
So I built a chamber and, around it, an automated rig that made the startle reproducible and locked it
to the optogenetics. The pneumatic and electronics build was done in collaboration with the Duke-NUS
3D Printing & Prototyping (3DPP) Lab; **credit to Dennis Ong**, who built the fabrication and
microcontroller side.

### The 17-lane climbing cassette

<!-- deposit photo here: the cassette diagram / photo (thesis Fig 1.2B) -->
![](cassette.png){.paper-figure fig-alt="The climbing cassette: 17 individual vertical lanes CNC-milled into a transparent acrylic sheet mounted on a white diffuser, each lane holding a single fly"}

[**Figure 1 — The climbing cassette.** The vertical arena: seventeen individual lanes, each housing a
single fly, milled into a transparent acrylic sheet backed by a white diffuser.]{.fig-legend}

Climbing behaviour was assessed in a custom acrylic cassette (170 × 94 × 9 mm) holding 17 individual
chambers (7 W × 86 H × 3 D mm), CNC-milled into a 3 mm transparent acrylic sheet mounted onto a white
acrylic diffuser. One fly per lane keeps individuals in frame and physically separated, so seventeen
animals can be recorded at once without ever crossing paths, and the tall, narrow geometry defines a
repeatable climb. Flies were briefly anaesthetised on ice for 30 s, loaded one to a chamber, and given
3 minutes to recover before any testing began.

### Illumination and imaging

<!-- deposit photo here: the setup schematic / green-LED stimulation (thesis Fig 1.2A, C) -->
![](illumination-setup.png){.paper-figure fig-alt="Illumination stack: an 850 nm infrared backlight through a diffuser for recording, plus green and blue optogenetic LEDs, with a near-infrared camera behind an 850 nm longpass filter"}

[**Figure 2 — Illumination and imaging.** Constant infrared backlight lets the camera record without
activating the opsins; separate green and blue LEDs deliver the optogenetic stimulation.]{.fig-legend}

The lighting has to do two jobs at once: light the arena well enough to track a fly, without that same
light tripping the light-sensitive opsins under test. Constant 850 nm infrared LEDs handle the
recording, invisible to the opsins, and an 850 nm longpass filter (Green.L 58-850) on the camera drops
any visible-light artefact. A white diffuser sits between the IR source and the arena to kill direct
reflections into the lens. Optogenetic stimulation is delivered separately by green (530 nm, Luxeon
Rebel SP-05-G4) or blue (460 nm, Luxeon Rebel SP-05-B4) LEDs on heatsinks (Luxeon Star N50-25B), driven
by a 700 mA BuckPuck (3023-D-E-700). Intensities were calibrated before each session with a Thorlabs
S130C photodiode and PM100D console, and set to 23.0 µW/mm² (green), 18.3 µW/mm² (blue) and
13.9 µW/mm² (red). Recording used a Chameleon3 near-infrared camera (FLIR CM3-U3-13S2C) with a
4.4–11 mm varifocal lens (Edmund Optics) at 5 fps, with seven LEDs 26 cm from the cassette providing
frontal illumination. The whole assembly sat inside a temperature-controlled incubator (Sanyo MIR-154).

### Automated pneumatic agitation

<!-- deposit photo here: the automated rig with pneumatic agitation system (thesis Fig 1.2D) -->
![](pneumatic-rig.png){.paper-figure fig-alt="The automated agitation rig: the climbing cassette on an acrylic backboard driven by a pneumatic piston on optical posts, with an Arduino control unit"}

[**Figure 3 — Automated agitation.** A pneumatic piston resets every fly to the chamber floor with
consistent force and timing, replacing manual tapping and synchronising the startle with tracking and
light delivery.]{.fig-legend}

Every recording set starts by displacing all seventeen flies to the bottom of their chambers. I did
this by hand at first, tapping the cassette down, but manual tapping introduced inconsistent delays
between the flies resetting and the start of tracking and light, which is exactly the temporal
precision an optogenetic experiment cannot afford to lose. So I replaced it with a pneumatic system
that agitates the same way every time and hands off cleanly to the recording.

The cassette mounts to an acrylic backboard (175 × 150 mm) with a 6.2 mm central hole for an M6 screw,
which secures a single-acting pneumatic piston (RS Components 176-1604) through a custom 3D-printed
piston-to-backboard adapter. The piston is carried on Thorlabs optical posts and further custom
3D-printed brackets to keep it rigidly aligned and damp vibration. Compressed air reaches the piston
through 3.5 mm tubing, regulated to 1 Pa at the source and gated by a normally-closed solenoid valve
(Monotaro 050 Series) with a manual emergency release.

Actuation is run by an Arduino UNO R3 (programmed by Dennis Ong, 3DPP Lab) driving the solenoid through
a relay (RS 783-0246, TE Connectivity). A start button extends the piston; after a set duration it
retracts, and the cycle repeats for the configured number of strokes. A small display and rotary encoder
let you set three parameters, push time (default 150 ms), pull time (default 150 ms), and stroke count
(default 5), with a green start and a red reset button. Tracking is paused throughout agitation and only
resumes on visual confirmation that every fly is back on the chamber floor, since a stroke can
occasionally fail to dislodge one.

### The assay protocol

Each fly runs through a fixed light sequence. Flies are agitated and allowed to climb for 3 s, then
recorded for 20 s in darkness (the dark phase). They are re-agitated, climb for 3 s in darkness, and are
then recorded for 20 s under LED activation, followed by 20 s of darkness (the recovery phase). The 3 s
lead-in guarantees the flies are already climbing when the recording window opens, so the readout is
climbing behaviour, not the startle itself.

### Tracking and analysis

Video and positions are processed in real time by CRITTA, a LabVIEW tracker that segments flies from
the background by subtraction, giving x–y coordinates per fly per frame. I then wrote a custom Python
package, [DrosoClimb](https://github.com/mnicolee/DrosoClimb), to turn those raw tracks into behaviour:
it converts coordinates to millimetres, splits each trial into its dark, light and recovery phases, and
classifies every frame as walking, pausing or falling using thresholds I calibrated against hand-labelled
ground-truth video. From there it computes the climbing metrics, speed, average height, bout speed and
number, fall count, straightness and maximum velocity, with effect sizes reported through the DABEST
estimation framework.

See the science this rig was built for → [the MBON climbing study](../../research.qmd).

[]{.section-rule}
